question.title: How to configure logging.properties to throw content into a Java class (without wrapper pattern)?
question.body:
I have a common logging.properties inside my tomcat/conf directory..

how can I modify it to be able to process (with a java class) all the log generated by my application?

Is there any way to do that without using a wrapper (from Log API) class??

answers.body:
You can use a Handler subclass to process the data being logged.

Then you can add it to your root logger via your config file or programmaticaly via the addHandler method of Logger class.

For example you can create a Handler subclass FoobarHandler that print all logged data on the console prefixed with a "Foobar " text:

public class MyHandler extends Handler {

    @Override
    public void close() throws SecurityException {
        // housekeeping before handler close
    }

    @Override
    public void flush() {
        // not really needed as data is processed without any sort of buffering
    }

    @Override
    public void publish(LogRecord record) {
        System.out.println("Foobar " + record.getMessage().toUpperCase());
    }

}

In your config file you add the class name (i.e. myapp.FoobarHandler) of your Handler to the global handlers config:

# Global logging properties.
# ------------------------------------------
# The set of handlers to be loaded upon startup.
# Comma-separated list of class names.
# (? LogManager docs say no comma here, but JDK example has comma.)
handlers=java.util.logging.FileHandler, myapp.FoobarHandler

# Default global logging level.
# Loggers and Handlers may override this level
.level=INFO

# Loggers
# ------------------------------------------
# Loggers are usually attached to packages.
# Here, the level for each package is specified.
# The global level is used by default, so levels
# specified here simply act as an override.
myapp.ui.level=ALL
myapp.business.level=CONFIG
myapp.data.level=SEVERE
